raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "AB 1042 will ensure the Department of Pesticide Regulation (DPR) has the authority to regulate pesticides used to treat seeds.",
    "AB 363 will ensure DPR finishes its evaluation of neonicotinoid pesticides, which are incredibly harmful to bees, butterflies, and other pollinators.",
    "Pesticides used on seeds are unregulated and unmonitored, leaving Californians without protections.",
    "A huge volume of pesticide use in California is completely unknown.",
    "Pesticides used on seeds have serious implications for pollinator health, water quality, and exposure to unregulated toxics.",
    "Data by the California Department of Food and Agriculture shows that, between 2011 and 2021, dozens of pesticide products not approved for use in California appeared on seeds.",
    "Systemic pesticides can stay in the plants throughout their life cycle, with 95% or more of the active ingredients typically remaining in the soil.",
    "Neonicotinoids are the world’s most widely used insecticides and are incredibly toxic to insects.",
    "One square foot of grass treated with a typical neonic lawn product can contain enough neonics to kill one million bees.",
    "Most neonic chemicals stay in the soil for years and are easily carried by rain or lawn watering.",
    "Neurotoxic neonic pesticides show up in urban waterways and the bodies of pregnant women in California.",
    "DPR has failed to look at the impacts of neonic pesticide use in urban and suburban areas where neonic water pollution is often the highest.",
    "Neonicotinoids and their use on seeds are some of the gravest threats to California’s birds."
  ]
}
```
